title: Project Orama
subtitle: Autonomous AI Drone — Mechanical Frame Design
summary: A collaborative interdisciplinary project to design and build an autonomous drone capable of detecting obstacles using a Raspberry Pi camera. I served on the mechanical sub-team, designing the drone frame using PETG carbon fiber filament and generative design in Fusion 360.
description:
Project Orama was a collaborative project where students from various backgrounds worked together to build an autonomous drone capable of detecting obstacles using a Raspberry Pi camera. The project was organized into three main sub-teams — Mechanical, Controls, and Electrical — and I was a member of the mechanical team.

My primary responsibility was designing the drone frame that would house all of the electronic components. I fabricated frame components using PETG carbon fiber filament via 3D printing, and used generative design tools in Fusion 360 to create an optimized drone arm capable of withstanding the load from the motors.

The two-layer design placed the Raspberry Pi, AI camera, and UPS (Uninterruptible Power Supply) on the first layer, while the ESC, flight controller board, and battery occupied the second layer. I also performed FEA statics and frequency simulations to identify failure-prone areas under max thrust conditions.

Overall, the project was a strong learning experience in mechanical design for autonomous systems, particularly in applying generative design and FEA to a real hardware constraint problem. Although the drone did not achieve flight within the project timeline due to size constraints, the design goals for the frame were largely met.
highlights: Designed a two-layer drone frame meeting a 100g weight target with a 12" diagonal motor span
Applied generative design in Fusion 360 to create lightweight, optimized drone arms from PETG CF
Performed statics and frequency FEA simulations at max motor thrust of 20N
3D printed structural components using PETG carbon fiber filament; assembled with heated inserts and FPV standoffs
Collaborated across Mechanical, Controls, and Electrical sub-teams

title: Bladesmithing — Lockback Folding Knife
subtitle: Mechanics of Materials · Spring Steel · Heat Treatment
summary: A semester-long group project for a Mechanics of Materials course. The goal was to design and fabricate a functional knife from a chosen steel, applying principles from material science and mechanics — including material selection, heat treatment, and final mechanical testing.
description:
As part of an ENGR 42 (Mechanics of Materials) course project, my group was tasked with designing and fabricating a working knife entirely from scratch. The project spanned the full semester and required planning every step of the process: design, material selection, and developing a heat treatment schedule based on published data.

Our group selected spring steel as our material of choice and used it to create a lockback folding knife. The heat treatment process followed the classical sequence of annealing, quenching, and tempering — each step carefully carried out to achieve the target hardness while avoiding brittleness.

At the end of the semester, the finished knife was evaluated through a series of tests including Rockwell Hardness (HRC) measurement, a tensile test of the material, and functional tests including chopping and shearing. The project integrated classroom theory around stress, material properties, and failure modes into a tangible, hands-on deliverable.
highlights: Designed and fabricated a lockback folding knife from spring steel as part of a team
Developed a full heat treatment schedule: annealing, quenching, and tempering
Applied Mechanics of Materials concepts including stress analysis and material property selection
Final knife evaluated for HRC hardness, tensile strength, chopping, and shearing performance

title: MATLAB / Raspberry Pi LED Matrix
subtitle: Orientation-Controlled Image Display on Sensehat
summary: Developed a MATLAB program that displays a custom image on an 8×8 LED matrix connected to a Raspberry Pi Sensehat. The program dynamically rotates the image based on the physical orientation of the Raspberry Pi and input from the onboard joystick.
description:
For this project in ENGR 7, I developed a MATLAB program capable of rendering a custom image on the 8×8 LED matrix of a Raspberry Pi Sensehat board. The core challenge was making the display dynamic — the image needed to rotate automatically based on the physical orientation of the device and respond to joystick input from the Sensehat.

The project involved interfacing MATLAB with the Raspberry Pi hardware, reading orientation data from the onboard sensors, and mapping that data to corresponding rotations of the LED matrix output. The joystick input added an additional layer of real-time interaction.

A full project report and a Raspberry Pi setup guide were produced as deliverables, documenting both the code design and the hardware configuration process.
highlights: Built a MATLAB program to render and rotate a custom image on an 8×8 LED matrix
Integrated Raspberry Pi Sensehat orientation sensors to drive real-time image rotation
Implemented joystick-based interaction on the Sensehat for additional user control
Produced a full ENGR 7 project report and a Raspberry Pi setup and user guide

title: MATLAB Tiny House Thermal Simulation
subtitle: Insulation Analysis via MATLAB & Simulink
summary: Used MATLAB and Simulink to simulate the thermal conditions of a tiny house based on real climate data. Produced a final IEEE-format report analyzing which insulation type performed best across a selection of tiny home configurations.
description:
This project involved using MATLAB and Simulink to model and simulate the thermal behavior of a tiny house under real-world climate conditions. Climate data was fed into the simulation to evaluate how different insulation types affected interior temperature regulation and energy efficiency.

The simulation compared several tiny home configurations with different insulation materials, and the results were analyzed to determine which combination offered the best thermal performance under the given climate inputs.

Findings were written up in a formal IEEE-standard report, providing a structured technical analysis of the simulation methodology, assumptions, results, and conclusions.
highlights: Modeled tiny house thermal dynamics using MATLAB and Simulink with real climate data
Compared multiple insulation types across different tiny home configurations
Identified the best-performing insulation option based on simulated thermal efficiency
Produced a final IEEE-format technical report documenting the full analysis
